label: Mexico City — Zócalo
sub: The political heart of Mexico. Site of presidential inaugurations, mass protests, and the 1968 student movement's rallies. PRI governed the DF as an appointed regency until 1997 when Cuauhtémoc Cárdenas became the first elected head of government.

label: Tlatelolco — Plaza de las Tres Culturas
sub: Site of the October 2, 1968 massacre where government forces killed student protesters. Death toll disputed (30–300+). A defining moment of PRI authoritarian repression, occurring 10 days before the Mexico City Olympics.

label: Monterrey — PAN Industrial Base
sub: Nuevo León's industrial capital and the heartland of Mexico's conservative business community. PAN has won multiple gubernatorial races here. Monterrey's Grupo Monterrey (ALFA, CEMEX, FEMSA) historically backed PAN and the opposition to PRI statism.

label: Guadalajara — PAN Stronghold
sub: Jalisco's capital and Mexico's second-largest city. Traditionally conservative Catholic culture made it a PAN heartland. PAN governed Jalisco repeatedly. Guadalajara's business community strongly backed the 1988 opposition movement.

label: Baja California — First PAN State
sub: Ernesto Ruffo Appel (PAN) became the first opposition governor in modern Mexican history in 1989, winning Baja California. This was the first crack in PRI's monopoly on state governments and a precedent for the 2000 democratic transition.

label: Chihuahua — PAN Northern Corridor
sub: PAN won the Chihuahua governorship in 1992 under Francisco Barrio after the 1986 election was widely considered stolen. The northern border states — Baja California, Chihuahua, Sonora — were the early proving ground for PAN's electoral viability.

label: Guanajuato — PAN Stronghold
sub: Vicente Fox's home state and a PAN gubernatorial stronghold since 1991. Fox served as Guanajuato governor 1995–1999 before running for president. Guanajuato has been governed by PAN continuously since Carlos Medina Plascencia's appointment in 1991.

label: León — PAN Municipal Heartland
sub: León, Guanajuato — Mexico's shoe-manufacturing hub — has been a PAN municipal stronghold since the 1980s. Conservative Catholic values and small-business culture made PAN the natural political home for this city's electorate.

label: San Cristóbal de las Casas — Zapatista Uprising
sub: On January 1, 1994 — the day NAFTA took effect — the EZLN captured San Cristóbal de las Casas and declared war on the Mexican state. The uprising drew global attention to indigenous rights and NAFTA's impact on rural Mexico. Peace talks followed in San Andrés Larráinzar.

label: Oaxaca — PRI Stronghold / PRD Uprising 2006
sub: Oaxaca was a PRI bastion for decades but also the site of the 2006 teachers' strike and APPO uprising that paralyzed the state for months. PRD later won the governorship in 2010. The state's indigenous diversity and poverty made it a focus of left-wing organizing.

label: Veracruz — PRI Stronghold / Corruption Scandal
sub: Veracruz was a traditional PRI stronghold. Governor Javier Duarte (2010–2016) fled the country amid massive corruption charges involving $2.5 billion in state funds. He was extradited from Guatemala and convicted in 2018 — a symbol of PRI-era gubernatorial impunity.

label: Tabasco — AMLO's Home State
sub: AMLO's birth state and political base. Tabasco was historically a PRI stronghold that became Morena's core territory. AMLO governed as the state's political patron since his failed 1994 gubernatorial campaign. Morena dominates Tabasco and uses it as a model of social program distribution.

label: Iguala, Guerrero — Ayotzinapa Disappearances
sub: On September 26–27, 2014, 43 students from Ayotzinapa Rural Teachers' College were forcibly disappeared in Iguala. The case exposed collusion between local authorities, the Guerreros Unidos cartel, and the federal government. It became the defining human rights crisis of Peña Nieto's presidency.

label: Michoacán — Cardenismo & AMLO Base
sub: Birthplace of Lázaro Cárdenas and his son Cuauhtémoc. Michoacán was a PRD stronghold and became a Morena base. The state faced severe cartel violence from La Familia Michoacana and Knights Templar. AMLO's alliance with 'autodefensas' (vigilante groups) reflected his approach to organized crime.

label: Estado de México — PRI Fortress
sub: Mexico's most populous state and the traditional core of PRI power. Peña Nieto governed it 2005–2011. Estado de México resisted Morena until 2023 when Delfina Gómez won — a seismic shift that signaled PRI's national collapse. The state was a key battleground in every presidential election.

label: Ciudad Juárez — Drug War Epicenter
sub: Under Calderón's drug war, Ciudad Juárez became the world's most violent city (2008–2011) with over 3,000 murders in 2010 alone. The Sinaloa vs. Juárez Cartel war, exacerbated by military deployment, made it a symbol of the drug war's human cost. Pan governed Chihuahua during this period.

label: Tijuana — Border Politics & PAN Stronghold
sub: Mexico's largest border city and a longstanding PAN electoral territory. Tijuana's commercial and cross-border economy made it receptive to PAN's pro-market, anti-PRI message. The city also became a major migration and cartel corridor, complicating its governance.

label: Querétaro — PAN Governance Model
sub: Querétaro became a model of PAN governance under successive administrations. The state attracted foreign manufacturing investment and achieved high human development indicators. PAN's Querétaro model — stable governance, rule of law, FDI-friendly — was cited as proof the party could govern effectively.

label: Aguascalientes — PAN Municipal Success
sub: A mid-sized PAN stronghold in central Mexico. PAN governments in Aguascalientes were cited for municipal efficiency and transparent administration. The city's annual San Marcos fair became a symbol of PAN's cultural conservatism governing alongside economic modernization.

label: Mérida — PAN Yucatán Stronghold
sub: Yucatán's capital and a PAN-controlled state since 2001. Víctor Cervera Pacheco's PRI hold broke when PAN's Patricio Patrón won in 2001. Mérida consistently ranks as one of Mexico's safest and best-governed cities, cited by PAN as proof of its governance model.

label: Chiapas — PRI Redoubt & EZLN Territory
sub: Mexico's southernmost and most indigenous state. PRI maintained control through clientelist networks among indigenous communities while the Zapatistas controlled autonomous zones. Morena eventually broke through in 2018. The San Andrés Larráinzar accords (1996) were signed here but never fully implemented.

label: Puebla — PRI to Morena Swing State
sub: Puebla was a PRI stronghold that became a Morena target. Governor Martha Érika Alonso (PAN) died in a helicopter crash in 2018, leading to a Morena victory in the 2019 election. Puebla's swing from PRI to Morena reflects the realignment of Mexico's political geography.

label: Sonora — Border State PRI/PAN Contest
sub: Northern border state that oscillated between PRI and PAN control. Claudia Pavlovich (PRI) governed 2015–2021 before Morena's Alfonso Durazo won in 2021 — reflecting the national shift. Sonora's border economy, mining, and agriculture made it a key swing state.

label: Sinaloa — PRI Base & Cartel Territory
sub: Sinaloa remained a PRI stronghold even as cartel power grew under the Sinaloa Cartel (Guzmán, Mayo Zambada). The convergence of PRI political networks and cartel territorial control made Sinaloa a model of narco-political coexistence. Rubén Rocha (Morena) finally broke PRI's hold in 2021.

label: Campeche — Oil State & Morena Base
sub: Campeche's offshore oil production (Cantarell field) made it strategically vital. The state shifted from PRI to Morena in 2021. AMLO's energy policy — prioritizing PEMEX — resonated in oil-dependent Campeche. The Dos Bocas refinery project in nearby Tabasco was AMLO's signature infrastructure initiative.

label: Guerrero — PRD Stronghold / Morena Territory
sub: Historically a PRD stronghold and one of Mexico's poorest states. Guerrero was devastated by the dirty war (Lucio Cabañas guerrilla movement), endemic cartel violence, and the 2014 Ayotzinapa crisis. PRD governed multiple cycles before Morena's Evelyn Salgado won in 2021.

label: Zacatecas — PRD to Morena
sub: A mining state in central Mexico that the PRD governed before Morena. Ricardo Monreal (Morena founder) governed as PRD until 2004. Zacatecas represents the trajectory of the Mexican left from PRD to Morena as AMLO consolidated the political movement.

label: Coahuila — PRI Holdout
sub: One of Mexico's few remaining PRI-governed states in 2024. Coahuila's industrial economy (AHMSA steel, Chrysler assembly) and border location kept PRI networks strong. Manlio Fabio Beltrones and Rubén Moreira maintained PRI control even as the national party collapsed.

label: Hidalgo — PRI Bastion Turned Morena
sub: Hidalgo was one of PRI's most reliable states, dominated by the Murillo Karam political family. Morena broke through in 2020 when Julio Menchaca won — a significant symbolic defeat for PRI in its heartland.

label: Ayutla, Oaxaca — Indigenous Autonomy Movement
sub: Ayutla Mixe and surrounding Oaxacan municipalities created indigenous autonomous governments under usos y costumbres (customary law) — a practice the 1996 San Andrés Accords tried to enshrine nationally. Oaxaca's 418 municipalities using traditional governance are the largest experiment in indigenous autonomy in Latin America.

label: Jalisco — PAN Stronghold & JALISCO Nueva Generación
sub: Jalisco became a PAN-governed state and home to the CJNG cartel — the irony being that one of Mexico's most institutionally strong states hosts one of its most violent criminal organizations. Enrique Alfaro (Movimiento Ciudadano) broke PAN's hold in 2018, adding another dimension to Mexico's party fragmentation.
